name: Brahmananda Sarasvati (Shankaracharya of Jyotirmath)

dates_evidence.description: Jyotirmath Maṭha records and modern published biographies.

core_thesis: Brahmānanda Sarasvatī (1871–1953), Śaṅkarācārya of Jyotirmath from 1941 to 1953, is the modern revivalist face of orthodox Advaita Vedānta within the Daśanāmī monastic tradition. The project is the practical re-establishment of the long-vacant Jyotirmath seat and the oral transmission of Śaṅkara's *siddhānta* in mid-twentieth-century North Indian vernacular registers. Doctrinally he stands within the conservative Vivaraṇa line, transmitting positions whose canonical loci are: Brahman alone is real (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.1.14; Padmapāda, *Pañcapādikā* on the *Adhyāsa-Bhāṣya*); the world is *vyāvahārika* (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, *Adhyāsa-Bhāṣya*); the *jīva* is non-different from Brahman though appearing as *cidābhāsa* under avidyā (Prakāśātman, *Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa* on the *Adhyāsa-Bhāṣya*). *Brahma-jñāna* — prepared by *karma* and *upāsanā*, ripened by *śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana* (the canonical Vedāntic *sādhana-catuṣṭaya*; Sadānanda, [*Vedānta-Sāra* §§14–35](cite://ramanuja/vedanta-sara/14-35)) — is the unique means of *mokṣa*. His distinctive contribution is institutional and pedagogical rather than scholastic: the consolidation of a modern Jyotirmath disciple-line and the translation of high *siddhānta* into accessible Hindi *satsaṅga* discourse. Almost no formal Sanskrit *prakaraṇas* survive; the corpus is principally recorded discourses transmitted by disciples [specific discourse loci NOT YET RETRIEVED]. He is to be sharply distinguished from the seventeenth- to eighteenth-century Gauḍa-Brahmānanda Sarasvatī, author of the *Laghu-Candrikā* on the *Advaita-Siddhi*.

engaged_works.title: Recorded discourses
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; few formal treatises survive — corpus consists chiefly of recorded discourses.
engaged_works.summary: Collected Hindi *satsaṅga* discourses delivered by Brahmānanda Sarasvatī during his tenure (1941–1953) as Śaṅkarācārya of Jyotirmath, recorded by disciples and circulated in modern editions. Doctrinally the discourses present orthodox Vivaraṇa-line Advaita in the vernacular pedagogical register: the unreality of the world as *vyāvahārika* (the canonical position at Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, *Adhyāsa-Bhāṣya*); the analytic non-difference of *jīva* and Brahman (Śaṅkara on *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya* 4.4.20; *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4); the roles of *karma*, *upāsanā*, *bhakti*, and *jñāna* in the path to *mokṣa* (Śaṅkara, *Bhagavad-Gītā-Bhāṣya* introduction; *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 3.4); the discipline of *śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana* (Sureśvara, [*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 4.41–44](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.41-44); the canonical formulation drawn from *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad* 2.4.5); the necessity of a qualified *guru* (Śaṅkara, [*Upadeśa-Sāhasrī* 1.1–4](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/1.1-4); *Vivekacūḍāmaṇi*, traditionally ascribed). They matter primarily as a witness to twentieth-century Daśanāmī Advaita pedagogy and to the transmission of orthodox *siddhānta* outside the technical Sanskrit *śāstra*-idiom. Specific discourse-locus citations within the modern Hindi corpus are [NOT YET RETRIEVED]. The corpus is foundational for several modern lineages claiming descent from the Jyotirmath seat. (NB: this is the modern Jyotirmath Śaṅkarācārya, distinct from the seventeenth-century Gauḍa-Brahmānanda Sarasvatī who authored the *Laghu-Candrikā*.)
